chapters.title: The Quartz Crisis
chapters.quote: In the early 1970s disaster struck the Swiss watch industry.
chapters.caption: Japanese competition + Bretton Woods collapse + quartz made mechanical timekeeping irrelevant.

chapters.title: The Golden Age (1945–1970)
chapters.quote: The best watches of the golden age have a quiet perfection that has never been equalled since.
chapters.caption: Thinness and accuracy. The holy trinity. Form followed function.

chapters.title: Golden Ellipse (1968)
chapters.quote: Patek expanded the size of the brand from 8 square millimeters to 800.
chapters.caption: First deliberate case-as-brand move. Beautiful but the crown became almost unusable.

chapters.title: Branding vs. Good Design
chapters.quote: Branding is centrifugal; design is centripetal.
chapters.caption: The fundamental conflict once real performance differences disappear.

chapters.title: Royal Oak (1972)
chapters.quote: Introducing steel at the price of gold.
chapters.caption: Gérald Genta’s integrated bracelet watch proved the new rules could work.

chapters.title: Nautilus (1976)
chapters.quote: In the Nautilus we really see the incompatibility of branding and design. It was huge.
chapters.caption: 42 mm. Ears. Instantly recognizable from across the room.

chapters.title: 3919 Hobnail Calatrava
chapters.quote: The ibankers bought the full mechanical story.
chapters.caption: The watch that finally turned Patek’s sales around with yuppie bankers.

chapters.title: The Brand Age Begins
chapters.quote: Since 1985 we’ve been in the brand age.
chapters.caption: Watches as status symbols and carefully managed asset bubbles.

chapters.title: Time Traveler’s Shock
chapters.quote: A surprisingly large number of them looked like a specific brand of bulky watch he was already familiar with.
chapters.caption: Giant watches, conglomerates, strange shapes, and you can’t just buy the top models.

chapters.title: Rolex & Luxury Jeeps
chapters.quote: Thick, tough watches made out of gold as well as steel. The result was a sort of luxury Jeep.
chapters.caption: Rolex understood branding earlier than almost anyone else.

chapters.title: Artificial Scarcity
chapters.quote: You can’t just buy a Nautilus. You have to spend years proving your loyalty first.
chapters.caption: Waiting lists, loyalty purchases, policing the secondary market. Managing the bubble.

chapters.title: The Comb-Over Effect
chapters.quote: A series of individually small changes takes you from something that’s a little bit off to something that’s freakishly wrong.
chapters.caption: Companies that rebuy their own watches to protect scarcity. The strangeness of no real function.

chapters.title: Follow the Problems
chapters.quote: The way to find golden ages is not to go looking for them. It is by following interesting problems.
chapters.caption: The single principle that saves you from brand work and finds the real golden ages.
